skill.name: brainstorming
skill.description: You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation.
skill.summary: A collaborative design methodology skill that helps turn ideas into fully formed designs through natural dialogue, questions, and iterative validation.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged external commands and cryptographic algorithms, but manual review confirms these are false positives. The skill contains only documentation text - no executable code. Backticks at line 38 are markdown formatting, not shell execution. No cryptographic code exists in the skill.

high_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
high_findings.description: Static analyzer incorrectly flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' at multiple lines. Manual review confirms no cryptographic code exists - the skill contains only prose documentation about design methodology.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill file contains no cryptographic code - only documentation text. Static analyzer pattern matching appears to have triggered on keywords in the prose.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby/Shell Backtick Execution
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' at line 38, but the backticks are markdown code formatting in a documentation path reference, not actual shell execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains a markdown path reference with backtick formatting - this is documentation, not executable code.

content.value_statement: This skill helps AI assistants turn vague ideas into concrete designs through structured dialogue, ensuring requirements are clear before implementation begins.
actual_capabilities: Guide users through collaborative idea refinement using one question at a time
Present design options with trade-offs and recommendations
Break designs into manageable sections for incremental validation
Generate structured design documents in markdown format
Explore multiple approaches before settling on a solution
limitations: Does not execute code or perform technical implementation
Does not access external APIs or services
Requires user participation through answering questions
Cannot validate technical feasibility without project context

use_cases.title: New Feature Planning
use_cases.description: When a user wants to add a new feature, guide them through understanding requirements, constraints, and success criteria before any implementation begins.

use_cases.title: Component Design Sessions
use_cases.description: Help design new UI components or backend services by asking targeted questions, presenting options, and iterating on the design through dialogue.

use_cases.title: Architecture Exploration
use_cases.description: Explore different architectural approaches for a system, presenting trade-offs and recommendations while validating understanding at each step.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Design Discussion
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to build something new. Can you help me brainstorm the design? The idea is: [describe your idea]. What questions do you have to understand what I am building?

prompt_templates.title: Explore Approaches
prompt_templates.prompt: We are designing [feature/system]. Based on what we discussed, what approaches would you recommend? Please present 2-3 options with trade-offs.
prompt_templates.scenario: When ready to explore design options

prompt_templates.title: Validate Design Section
prompt_templates.prompt: Here is my proposed design for [section]: [design content]. Does this look right? What should I clarify or change?

prompt_templates.title: Generate Design Document
prompt_templates.prompt: We have validated the design. Can you write this up as a design document at docs/plans/[topic]-design.md?

output_examples.input: I want to add a user notification system to my app
output_examples.output: Great! Let me understand what you are building. First question: What is the primary purpose of these notifications - are they for user activity (like comments, likes), system alerts (maintenance, errors), or something else?

output_examples.input: What if I want push notifications too?
output_examples.output:
Good point. I have two approaches to consider:

1. **Single channel first**: Start with in-app notifications, add push later when you have more resources.
2. **Multi-channel from start**: Use a service like OneSignal or Firebase that handles both.

My recommendation is approach 1 if you are early stage - it keeps complexity down. Which direction interests you more?

best_practices: Ask one question per message to avoid overwhelming the user
Prefer multiple choice questions when possible for faster responses
Present your recommendation first, then explain reasoning
Validate understanding incrementally by presenting design sections
anti_patterns: Asking multiple questions in a single message
Jumping to implementation without validating the design first
Presenting only one option without exploring alternatives
Ignoring user feedback and continuing with the original plan

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use this skill whenever you need to create something new - a feature, component, or system. It helps ensure requirements are clear before implementation begins.

faq.question: Does this skill write code?
faq.answer: No, this skill focuses on design and planning. After the design is validated, you can use other skills like implementation planning or git worktrees to proceed.

faq.question: How many questions should I ask?
faq.answer: Ask one question per message. If a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple sequential questions.

faq.question: What if the user is unclear about their requirements?
faq.answer: Ask clarifying questions to understand the purpose, constraints, and success criteria. Focus on the 'why' before the 'what'.

faq.question: Should I always present multiple approaches?
faq.answer: Yes, propose 2-3 approaches with trade-offs. Lead with your recommendation but explain why alternatives might also work.

faq.question: How do I know when the design is complete?
faq.answer: The design is complete when you have covered architecture, components, data flow, error handling, and testing - and the user has validated each section.
